coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional carbon quantum dots as a theranostic nanomedicine for fluorescence imaging-guided glutathione depletion to improve chemodynamic therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jun
Hu, Zu-E
We, Yun-Jie
Liu, Yan-Hong
Wang, Na
Yu, Xiao-Qi
coredata.dc:description: To minimize unwanted reactions with high concentrations of reduced glutathione (GSH) in the tumor microenvironment (TME) during chemodynamic therapy (CDT), a simple and effective strategy was developed to fabricate a TME stimuli-responsive theranostic nanomedicine (Fe-CD) for fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion and cancer therapy by combining fluorescent imaging carbon dots (CD) and Fe(III). Introducing Fe(III) into Fe-CD not only quenched the fluorescence of CD while reacting with and consuming intracellular GSH for fluorescence imaging of the depletion of GSH but also provided a source of metal ions to generate more abundant hydroxyl radicals (•OH) with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) through the Fenton reaction to improve CDT. Fe-CD showed promising •OH generation under H2O2 to effectively degrade methylene blue in vitro and obviously activate the green fluorescence of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) probe in cells. Benefiting from the fluorescence enhancement in response to TME stimulation, Fe-CD greatly enhanced CDT cytotoxicity while monitoring successful GSH depletion by fluorescence imaging. Fe-CD has the potential to act as a theranostic nanomedicine for fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion to amplify CDT.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence imaging
Glutathione depletion
Chemodynamic therapy
Theranostic nanomedicine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: •OH generation activated by H2O2 in vitro
Fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion in vitro
Fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion in cells
Fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion in cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis routes of Fe-CD by a two-step method. (B) Fluorescence spectra and (C) maximum emission intensity at 440 nm of 100 µg mL-1 CD in the presence of different concentrations of Fe(III) after 10 min. (D) Fe(III) release rate of Fe-CD over 24 h when the concentrations of CD and Fe(III) were 2.5 mg mL−1 and 25 mM, respectively. Data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3).

(A) Particle size distribution histogram, (B) TEM image, (C) Fe 2p spectra from the XPS results for Fe-CD. (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (E) fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of Fe-CD and CD. (F) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of Fe-CD.

(A) Schematic diagram of fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion. (B) Fluorescence enhancement curve of the Fe-CD after adding different concentrations of GSH for 10 min. (C) Fluorescence enhancement curve of the Fe-CD after adding 10 mM GSH for different incubation times. (D) FTIR spectrum and (E) Fe 2p XPS spectra of the Fe-CD after adding 1 mM GSH.

Degradation curve of MB with the addition of (A) 500 µM H2O2 and (B) 2 mM H2O2. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of MB under different conditions after 25 min. (D) ESR spectra of only 500 µM H2O2, only Fe-CD, and Fe-CD and 500 µM H2O2 with DMPO as the spin trap. The final concentration of CD in Fe-CD was 50 µg mL−1.

CLSM images (A) and relative fluorescence intensity (B) of CD in cellular uptake. The scale bar is 20 μm. Data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3). The asterisk (*) denotes statistical significance relative to the 4 h group: **P < 0.01, and ****P < 0.0001.

(A) CLSM images of A549 cells treated with Fe-CD under conditions of additional or depletion of GSH. (B) GSH content of A549 cells treated with CD and Fe-CD. The scale bar is 20 μm. Data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3).
CLSM images of ROS generation in Fe-CD at (A) pH 7.4 and (B) pH 5.5 in cells. The scale bar is 20 μm.

Cell viability of A549 cells treated with CD and Fe-CD (A) without or (B) with 500 µM H2O2. Data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3). The asterisk (*) denotes statistical significance: *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, and ****P < 0.0001.
IC50 (µg mL−1) values and enhancing effect of CD, Fe-CD and Fe-CD + 500 µM H2O2.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated based on the ratio of IC50 values of CD and Fe-CD.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated based on the ratio of IC50 values of Fe-CD with and without H2O2. Data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Multifunctional carbon quantum dots as a theranostic nanomedicine for fluorescence imaging-guided glutathione depletion to improve chemodynamic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry, Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To minimize unwanted reactions with high concentrations of reduced glutathione (GSH) in the tumor microenvironment (TME) during chemodynamic therapy (CDT), a simple and effective strategy was developed to fabricate a TME stimuli-responsive theranostic nanomedicine (Fe-CD) for fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion and cancer therapy by combining fluorescent imaging carbon dots (CD) and Fe(III). Introducing Fe(III) into Fe-CD not only quenched the fluorescence of CD while reacting with and consuming intracellular GSH for fluorescence imaging of the depletion of GSH but also provided a source of metal ions to generate more abundant hydroxyl radicals (•OH) with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) through the Fenton reaction to improve CDT. Fe-CD showed promising •OH generation under H2O2 to effectively degrade methylene blue in vitro and obviously activate the green fluorescence of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) probe in cells. Benefiting from the fluorescence enhancement in response to TME stimulation, Fe-CD greatly enhanced CDT cytotoxicity while monitoring successful GSH depletion by fluorescence imaging. Fe-CD has the potential to act as a theranostic nanomedicine for fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion to amplify CDT.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a series of complex diseases that lead to uncontrolled cell growth and death and that have the ability to escape the body's natural cellular mechanisms [1–2]. Recent statistics have shown a significant increase in cancer-related mortality [3]. The current mainstream cancer treatments (such as surgery, chemotherapy and radiation therapy) show only limited results in terms of curing cancer. There is an urgent need for advanced cancer therapeutic strategies with improved efficacy and reduced side effects. Since chemodynamic therapy (CDT) was first proposed in 2016 [4], it has been widely used and exploited as a spatially controllable and specific therapeutic approach. Under the catalysis of transition metal atoms or ions in CDT nanomedicine, the overexpressed hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in tumors produces more cytotoxic hydroxyl radicals (•OH) based on localized Fenton or Fenton-like reactions, which exert forceful effects on certain important biomacromolecules in cells, such as DNA and protein, to induce cell apoptosis [5–7]. Based on the above characteristics, an increasing number of CDT reagents are being developed to continuously expand the types of CDT and overcome the limitations presented by the tumor microenvironment (TME) [8–10].

Glutathione has two forms in organisms, oxidized glutathione (GSSG) and reduced glutathione (GSH) [11]. GSH is the main form of glutathione that is present in the body and performs biological functions. As an extremely important intracellular antioxidant and free radical scavenger in the body, GSH is involved in various redox physiological reactions that can help to maintain normal immune function [12,13]. In particular, GSH is overexpressed in tumor tissues (up to 10 mM) by at least 4 times the amount in normal tissues [14]. Numerous GSH-responsive nanocarriers constructed based on the characteristics of GSH overexpression have been reported in drug/gene delivery [15–19]. However, GSH counteracts against the massive amounts of •OH generated by CDT in an attempt to maintain redox homeostasis in cells, which limits the therapeutic effect of CDT [20,21]. The therapeutic promise of CDT depends on effectively preventing the undesired reaction with GSH to reduce its effects. Because of the unique features of GSH, much work is devoted to its depletion by means of chemical inhibitors [22,23], high-valence transition metals [24,25], double bonds [26], etc. Zhao et al. [27] synthesized nanoparticles based on l-buthioninesulfoximine (BSO), which inhibited the synthesis of GSH to realize excellent tumor ablation and ultimately a superior CDT effect. They also reported a ferric pyrophosphate nanoagent for synergetic photo/chemodynamic therapy based on the Fe(III) oxidation of GSH to GSSG for GSH depletion [28]. Similar results were observed by Qu et al. [29] who reported that the introduction of Cu2+ into g-C3N4 nanosheets enhanced the depletion of intracellular GSH levels. All these methods have achieved good GSH depletion to improve the efficiency of CDT. Additionally, GSH plays a key role in cell growth and metabolic maintenance, and monitoring the level of GSH in cancer cells is of great physiological significance [30–32]. Unfortunately, very little research focuses on integrating imaging and therapeutic functionalities in a single theranostic nanomedicine to visualize GSH depletion and improve CDT simultaneously.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), thanks to their superior properties,which include facile fabrication and surface functionalization, stable optical properties, and good biocompatibility, have attracted widespread attention for applications in the bioimaging of metal cations and biological molecules [33–35], drug/gene delivery [36], etc. Some CDs have a large specific surface area, which is advantageous for loading metal cations (Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, etc.) on the surface, and obvious fluorescence enhancement or quenching can be used to detect these metal cations. Park et al. [37] reported fluorescent colored CDs to quantify Fe3+ in biofluids and cells with high sensitivity. Gomes et al. [38] synthesized excitation-independent CDs through facile solvothermal methods, and the CDs showed high sensitivity for sensing endogenous Fe3+ in a stress-induced cell-based model. Additionally, some CDs induce the efficient quenching of fluorescence and then strongly interact with GSH to restore fluorescence, enabling the detection of GSH. In 2014, Xi et al. [39] reported Au(III)-decorated CDs that could detect GSH through an “off–on” fluorescence approach. Subsequently, the use of Fe3+, Cu2+ , etc. as quenchers was also developed to detect GSH based on a similar mechanism [40,41]. The above results indicate that CDs may easily and quickly form iron-containing nanoparticles with Fe3+ and quench their fluorescence, and GSH then continues to react with the nanoparticles to restore the fluorescence while oxidizing GSH to GSSG. Unfortunately, the idea of killing two birds with one stone for fluorescence imaging of GSH based on CDs has not been further developed and used, especially in the field of CDT.

Fe(II) or Fe(III) is the most commonly used metal cation in CDT, and GSH is an important factor that limits the effect of CDT in the body. CDs have emerged as promising candidates for designing a multifunctional theranostic nanomedicine for fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion to further improve CDT. Thus, we fabricated an Fe(III)-decorated CD (Fe-CD) through a simple two-step method with high Fe(III) loading. Fe(III) in Fe-CD reacted with GSH and consumed it, recovered the strong blue fluorescence of the CD, and achieved fluorescence imaging of GSH depletion in vitro and in cells. Furthermore, electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements and methylene blue (MB) degradation experiments indicated that Fe-CD generated highly toxic •OH in the presence of H2O2. Fe-CD thereby overcame the undesired reactions of overexpressed GSH in the TME, preventing GSH from hampering CDT, and achieved fluorescence imaging of the important physiological process of GSH depletion. Therefore, Fe-CD constructed by a facile two-step strategy is a promising theranostic nanomedicine for fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion to improve CDT.

Unless otherwise stated, all compounds and reagents were purchased and used directly without purification. Glycerol (EP, BP, USP grade), ferric chloride (98%), glutathione (98%), N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (95%, DAMO), and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (97%, DMPO) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Methylene blue (98.5%, MB) was ordered from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory. Both 1640 medium and foetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Invitrogen Corporation (USA). The A549 cell lines and dendritic cell lines were obtained from the Shanghai Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and mapping images were obtained via a Hitachi HT-7800. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a PXSAM 800 spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet Impact 410 FTIR spectrometer. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was performed using a NexION 350X ICP-MS spectrometer. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were characterized by a Bruker EMX plus. UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded using a TPersee Model TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer and a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer, respectively. Particle size was measured on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90.

The synthesis of CD was similar to the reported method [42]. Briefly, 1.5 mL of DAMO and 10 mL of glycerol were added to the reactor and degassed with nitrogen for 2 min. After 12 h of solvothermal reaction at 260 °C, the cooled solid residue was added to 20 mL of DI water and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to collect the dispersion. The dispersion was dialyzed against DI water with a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1 kDa) for 2 days to remove the low-molecular-weight substances. Finally, the CD dispersion was freeze-dried to obtain CD as a brown oily liquid and stored at room temperature for use.

1 mL of 50 mM ferric chloride aqueous solution and 1 mL of 5 mg mL-1 CD dispersion were added to a 2 mL dialysis tube (MWCO 100–500 Da) with a final CD concentration of 2.5 mg mL−1, and the concentration of ferric chloride in the tube was 25 mM. Then, 15 mL of DI water was placed outside the tube to remove noninteracting iron ions. After full mixture and interaction for 24 h, the Fe-CD in the dialysis tube was removed and stored at 4 °C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: •OH generation activated by H2O2 in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The generation of •OH activated by H2O2 was visualized by the UV–vis absorption of MB. A mixture of Fe-CD and 10 μg/mL MB was added to 2 mM or 0.5 mM H2O2, and the final concentration of CD in Fe-CD was 50 µg mL−1. At predetermined time intervals, the dispersion was characterized via UV–vis spectrometry. The samples were recorded from 500 nm to 800 nm.

The radical scavenger DMPO was used to detect the generation of •OH in vitro with ESR spectroscopy to further explore the mechanism. Fe-CD, 0.5 mM H2O2 and 1% DMPO were mixed quickly, the resulting dispersion was immediately transferred to a quartz capillary tube, and the ESR signals were measured at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Fe-CD for fluorescence enhancement was prepared with different concentrations of GSH (from 0 mM to 20 mM) and different incubation times (from 0 min to 240 min), and the final concentration of CD in Fe-CD was 50 µg mL−1. At predetermined time intervals, the dispersion was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer. The excitation wavelength was 350 nm, and the emission spectra were collected from 380 nm to 680 nm.

For GSH depletion, a near-infrared hemicyanine-based probe (HXPIS) was used to detect biological thiols in living cells reported by our group [43]. GSH (1 mM) with or without Fe-CD was placed at room temperature for 30 min, 1 h and 4 h, and the final concentration of CD in the Fe-CD was 50 µg mL−1. Then, HXPIS (5 mM) was added, and the mixture was incubated for another 5 min and measured by a fluorescence spectrometer.
A549 cells and dendritic cells were cultured in 1640 medium with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics (penicillin–streptomycin, 10 kU mL−1) maintained in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2.

In brief, 5 × 104 cells were seeded and further incubated for 12 h. After that, each well was incubated for 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, and 12 h at a final CD concentration of 50 µg mL−1. The cells were incubated with NucRedTM Live 647 for 20 min after washing for three times with PBS. Cellular uptake was monitored by confocal laser-scanning microscopy (CLSM, Zeiss LSM 780 system, Germany). CLSM images were captured at excitation wavelengths of 405 nm for CD (blue) and 633 nm for NucRedTM Live 647 (red).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion in cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cells were seeded at 5 × 104 cells per well for 12 h, and one well was incubated with 10 mM GSH for 1 h as an exogenous supply. One well was further incubated with N-ethylmaleimide (NEM, 10 μM) for 20 min to consume the additional GSH as the control group. In another well, the medium was replaced by new cell culture medium for the same amount of time. After that, Fe-CD with a final CD concentration of 50 µg mL−1 was added and incubated for another 4 h. After replacement of the cell culture medium, the cells were washed three times with PBS. Due to the objective limitations of the instrument, we chose 405 nm as the excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments. CLSM images were captured at excitation wavelengths of 405 nm for Fe-CD (blue).

2,7-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was used as a probe for detecting reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells. First, 5 × 104 A549 cells were cultured per well for 12 h. Then, the cells were cultured for another 12 h under the following conditions at pH 7.4 or pH 5.5 (1 mL 1640 medium): H2O2 (500 μM), Fe-CD (50 µg mL-1 CD), and Fe-CD (50 µg mL-1 CD) with H2O2 (500 μM). After that, the cells were washed 3 times with PBS and incubated with DCFH-DA (15 μM) for 30 min. Finally, the •OH signal in cells was detected by CLSM under 488 nm excitation.

A549 cells and dendritic cells were cultured for 24 h in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells in 100 μL of 1640 medium per well). Then, different concentrations of CD or Fe-CD (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200 μg/mL) with or without H2O2 (500 μM) were added to the wells and incubated for another 24 h. After that, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing 10% Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) solution, and the wells were incubated for another 30 min. Finally, a microplate reader was used to measure the absorbance of each well at 450 nm. Cell viability (%) was evaluated according to the manufacturer’s instructions as follows: cell viability = (ODtreated/ODcontrol) × 100%.

Unless otherwise stated, the data were expressed as mean ± SD values based on triplicate independent measurements. Statistical analyses of data were performed using Student’s t-test. Differences of P < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

In this study, we introduced a facile method of using CD and iron ion complexes for CDT as shown in Fig. 1 A. First, CD was prepared by a solvothermal method. It has been reported that specific covalent binding occurs between Fe(III) and amine groups as well as hydroxyl groups.[44,45] Inspired by this, we deduced that Fe(III) could be modified effectively to obtain a complex (Fe-CD) coordinated with amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CD. CD was selected because of the nature of the nucleus, which ensures a suitable nanoparticle size and gives the nanomedicine fluorescence properties that can be used in fluorescence imaging. Fe(III), the source of the intracellular Fenton reaction catalyst, not only increased the efficiency of CDT but also easily formed Fe-CD to quench the fluorescence of CD. To verify the above assumptions, different concentrations of Fe(III) were added to explore the appropriate additional concentration. When Fe(III) was added, the fluorescence intensity of CD significantly decreased, as shown in the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1B. The fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing concentrations of Fe(III). This result preliminarily confirmed that Fe(III) could be used to quench the fluorescence intensity of CD. By quantifying the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm in Fig. 1C, it was found that at 1000 µM Fe(III), the decrease in fluorescence almost reached equilibrium. Therefore, we chose this concentration ratio as the target concentration of Fe(III) to interact with the CD to form Fe-CD. Further examination of the time of compounding in Fig. 1D shows that with extension of the interaction time, although the release rate of uncomplexed Fe(III) gradually increased, the total released amount was still<0.2%. This extremely small amount of Fe(III) was almost negligible. For better stability and more complete complexation, 24 h was chosen as the final interaction time between CD and Fe(III). When the concentration of CD was 2.5 mg mL−1, the concentration of Fe(III) was 25 mM, and the Fe(III) loading in Fe-CD was 0.56 mg after complexation for 24 h.

CD and Fe-CD were obtained with good aqueous dispersion, benefiting from the abundant hydroxyl and amino structures in glycerol and DAMP. After forming an aqueous dispersion, the particle size and morphology of Fe-CD were characterized by DLS and TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 A and 2B. DLS results showed that Fe-CD had a narrow size distribution with a polydispersity index of 0.203. The hydrodynamic diameter of Fe-CD was 24.3 nm. The TEM results also showed that Fe-CD exhibited uniform nanodots and had lattice fringes with a mean distance of 0.168 nm. Due to the presence of a hydration layer on Fe-CD, the hydrodynamic diameter obtained by DLS was larger than that obtained by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2C, the Fe 2p XPS peak shape and binding energies (709.9 and 724.7 eV for Fe 2p3/2and Fe 2p1/2, respectively) confirmed the presence of Fe(III) in Fe-CD. The basic optical properties of Fe-CD were also characterized as shown in Fig. 2D, 2E and 2F. A peak at 300 nm was appeared in the UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe-CD in Fig. 2D, and the spectra of Fe-CD did not coincide with the spectra of Fe(II) and CD but were relatively consistent with those of Fe(III). This observation was attributed to the characteristic absorption of Fe(III) and further confirmed the coordination state of Fe(III) with the Fenton reaction potential. Before and after the introduction of Fe, the excitation peak and emission peak showed no change in shift except for a significant decrease in intensity due to the strong quenching effect of Fe in Fig. 2E. Moreover, the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission feature under excitation from 300 nm to 420 nm was also found to be attributed to the surface state of Fe-CD in Fig. 2F.

Generally, CDs fluorescence is quenched by quenchers, and then the CDs strongly interact with GSH to restore the fluorescence, thus detecting the content of GSH. In our current work, the Fe(III) in Fe-CD was released not only as a quencher but also as an important factor in CDT after Fe-CD reacted with GSH. However, the complex of CD and Fe(II) was not stable when Fe(III) was reduced to Fe(II) by GSH.[46] GSH was converted into GSSG, while Fe(III) was simultaneously reduced to Fe(II) with higher CDT efficiency. This important process was also monitored by enhancement of the Fe-CD fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 3 A. The fluorescence emission intensity of Fe-CD at 450 nm gradually recovered with increasing concentrations of GSH and incubation time, as shown in Fig. 3B and 3C. After 4 h of incubation with 10 mM GSH, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CD was increased by a factor of 2.0 times, which provided the possibility of intracellular fluorescence imaging. According to the mechanism, the Fe(III) ions on the surface of Fe-CD were released after adding GSH, which led to a reduction in the hydrated particle size of Fe-CD as shown in Fig. S1.

To further investigate whether Fe(III) was reduced to Fe(II), causing GSH depletion in vitro, we first used a fluorescent probe that detected biological thiols to preliminarily explore the consumption of GSH, as shown in Fig. S2. CD with a high specific surface area would have to adsorb a certain number of molecules, resulting in a slight decrease in the fluorescence intensity due to insufficient contact between GSH and the probe. However, Fe-CD had significantly decreased fluorescence intensity under the same conditions. The initial intensity was reduced by 40.2% after 4 h at 1 mM GSH, which initially reflected the possibility of Fe-CD depleting GSH. The most important contributors to the antioxidant effect of GSH are the SH groups in its structure. The characteristic peak of SH groups in the FTIR spectrum is at 2522 cm−1 and is shown in Fig. 3D. After the addition of Fe-CD, this characteristic peak visibly disappeared, which suggested that a reaction occurred between Fe-CD and the SH groups in GSH. We used XPS to further qualitatively and quantitatively study the above phenomena in Fig. 3E. Similarly, approximately 45.6% of the Fe(III) in Fe-CD was reduced to Fe(II) after GSH was added. Based on the above data, we believed that the Fe(III) in Fe-CD could react with GSH through redox reactions and consume it, recovering the strong blue fluorescence of CD and achieving fluorescence imaging of the process of GSH depletion.

The key factor in CDT was whether Fe-CD reacted with H2O2 to produce •OH. We first intuitively explored this important step through MB degradation experiments. MB is a common blue dye that can be degraded by the •OH generated in the Fenton reaction and become colorless. As shown in the UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 4 A and 4B, regardless of whether Fe-CD was combined with 0.5 mM H2O2 or 2 mM H2O2, the UV–vis absorption of MB gradually decreased with increasing time. Consistent with the CDT mechanism, the absorption of MB decreased even more at higher H2O2 concentrations. In the control groups, only Fe-CD or only H2O2 was added, and MB was hardly degraded in these two groups. The degradation percentage of the control group was<4% after 25 min, as shown in Fig. 4C. However, when Fe-CD and 0.5 mM H2O2 or 2 mM H2O2 were placed in MB solution, MB was effectively degraded by 50.8% or 74.0%, respectively, after 25 min. This phenomenon benefited from the rapid Fenton reaction between Fe-CD and H2O2, which generated a large amount of •OH to degraded MB.

The MB degradation mechanism was verified by ESR spectra. DMPO, as a •OH scavenger, is oxidized by •OH in the Fenton reaction to produce DMPO-OH, resulting in a 1:2:2:1 quartet ESR characteristic signal. As shown in Fig. 4D, the abovementioned characteristic peaks were not generated in the control groups to which only H2O2 or only Fe-CD was added, which proved that there was no •OH in this system and that MB was not effectively degraded by the Fenton reaction. However, the signal of DMPO-OH was clearly observed in the mixed solution containing both H2O2 and Fe-CD. The results showed that a Fenton reaction occurred between H2O2 and Fe-CD to generate •OH. This reaction was also an important basis for CDT to play a role in tumor treatment.

As shown in Fig. 5 A, NucRedTM Live 647 was used to stain the nuclei of A549 cells. After incubation for different periods of time, the efficiency of cellular uptake was verified by blue fluorescence. First, CD was present in the cytoplasm even after incubation for 12 h. In the early stage of incubation, the blue fluorescence increased significantly with time and reached its peak at 4 h, which initially reflected the good efficiency of CD in cell uptake. Perhaps due to the efflux of cells, the fluorescence intensity not only failed to increase but decreased slightly after 12 h of incubation in cells. [47] Further quantification of the above CLSM images, as shown in Fig. 5B, revealed that the cellular uptake of CD after 4 h was significantly different from that after other incubation times in the cell uptake experiment, and its relative fluorescence intensity was 1.5 times, 1.1 times and 1.1 times that after 1 h, 2 h and 12 h, respectively, under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion in cells
ce:section.ce:para: To further visualize the characterization of Fe-CD in response to GSH in cells, we used its own fluorescence imaging-guided properties. Exogenous addition of GSH was performed to introduce a sufficient amount of GSH into the cells, and Fe-CD was then added. In the other group, NEM was added prior to the addition of Fe-CD to consume the previous addition of GSH. As shown in Fig. 6 A, significantly brighter blue fluorescence was observed by CLSM after the exogenous addition of GSH than after the addition of Fe-CD without GSH. This was consistent with the results obtained in the fluorescence-guided enhancement experiment in vitro, and it preliminarily confirmed that Fe-CD had the potential to guide the enhancement of the GSH content by the fluorescence signal in cells. Furthermore, when NEM was used to consume additional GSH in cells, the previously bright blue fluorescence became decreased. This further indicated that Fe-CD was indeed able to guide or diagnose the changes in GSH content by fluorescence imaging at the cellular level. We further explored the GSH-depleting ability of Fe-CD by using the GSH/GSSG assay kit in Fig. 6B. There was almost no change in the GSH content when the cells were treated with CD. However, Fe-CD, which introduced Fe(III), resulted in a significant 31.8% reduction in the GSH content in the cells. This result was consistent with the conclusions drawn from the in vitro experiments, indicating the great potential of Fe-CD for GSH depletion. The above phenomena were attributed to Fe-CD effectively consuming intracellular GSH. This process also restored the bright fluorescence of CD and qualitatively showed a decrease in intracellular GSH content through the enhancement of fluorescence. Based on this, these results laid a solid foundation for Fe-CD to achieve GSH depletion while guiding the process through fluorescence imaging.

Next, we further explored whether Fe-CD could conduct the Fenton reaction at the cellular level to act as a CDT nanomedicine. DCFH-DA was used to detect the ROS produced by Fe-CD in the presence of H2O2 at different pH values through CLSM. DCFH-DA is not a fluorescent substance. After it enters the cell and is hydrolysed by esterase to produce nonfluorescent DCFH, it can be oxidized by ROS to produce green fluorescent DCF . As shown in Fig. 7 A and Fig. S3, when either blank cells were added or after 500 µM H2O2 was added, more green fluorescence was not effectively observed at either pH 7.4 or pH 5.5. However, Fe-CD with 500 µM H2O2 exhibited a stronger ability to generate ROS than A549 cells with 500 µM H2O2 and Fe-CD at pH 7.4, and bright green fluorescence was observed. Fe-CD had a strong ability to generate •OH through localized Fenton reactions in cells. It is worth noting that Fe-CD still had brighter green fluorescence without added H2O2 at both pH 7.4 and pH 5.5 than blank A549 cells. This result also showed that Fe-CD used H2O2 overexpressed by tumor cells to perform the above transformation to produce ROS by CDT. According to reports in the literature, the tumor microenvironment is weakly acidic, and we further explored the effect of pH on the generation of ROS by CDT. As expected, ROS under acidic conditions had a stronger oxidizing ability and slightly improved the CDT of Fe-CD, as evidenced by the increase in green fluorescence in Fig. 7B. Subsequently, 1% tert-butanol (TBA) or 1% dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was used as specific scavenger for •OH, as shown in Fig. S4. When TBA or DMSO was added and incubated for 30 min, the CLSM images showed that the green fluorescence intensity of the scavenging group was significantly reduced. It was evident that the introduction of excellent Fe-CD effectively improved the generation of ROS under the action of CDT.

Furthermore, the CDT cytotoxicity of Fe-CD was further explored in cancer cells (A549 cells) and normal cells (dendritic cells). The small content of H2O2 in normal cells was not enough for effective CDT. After the dendritic cells were incubated with Fe-CD, the viability of dendritic cells was much higher than that of A549 cells at the same concentration, as shown in Fig. S5. Even at concentrations as high as 150 µg/ml, more than 80% of the cells survived after 24 h of incubation. This showed that Fe-CD had good biocompatibility with normal cells. CD showed relatively little cytotoxicity in cancer cells. However, once Fe-CD was constructed, more ROS could be generated to kill cancer cells, as shown in Fig. 8 A. Since the cell H2O2 level in the TME were relatively low, we added 500 µM H2O2 to further simulate a more realistic TME in Fig. 8B. The CDT cytotoxicity of Fe-CD was significantly enhanced after the addition of H2O2, and there were significant differences in all concentration intervals compared to those of the sample without the addition. The IC50 values according to SPSS analysis were 184 ± 16 µg mL−1, 128 ± 15 µg mL−1, and 30 ± 4 µg mL−1 for CD, Fe-CD and Fe-CD + 500 µM H2O2, respectively. As shown in Table 1 , Fe-CD increased that value by 1.4 times compared with CD, and Fe-CD with 500 µM H2O2 resulted in an IC50 that was 4.3 times higher than that of Fe-CD alone. The above results showed that Fe-CD exerted stronger CDT cytotoxicity through the Fenton reaction with H2O2, thereby inducing significant cell death.

In summary, we applied an “off–on” detection strategy to the nanomedicine field for fluorescence imaging-guided reduced glutathione (GSH) depletion to improve chemodynamic therapy (CDT). A facile strategy was developed to fabricate tumor microenvironment (TME) stimuli-responsive theranostic nanomedicine (Fe-CD). Fe(III) in Fe-CD reacted with GSH by redox reactions and consumed it, which recovered the strong fluorescence of CD to realize fluorescence imaging of the process of GSH depletion. Moreover, the reduced Fe(II) had a strong Fenton catalytic ability to generate more reactive oxygen species (ROS), showing promise for CDT cytotoxicity in cancer cells. Our work provides a new strategy to construct theranostic nanomedicine for fluorescence imaging-guided GSH depletion and H2O2-triggered ROS generation and will broaden the applications of carbon quantum dots in the field of CDT. Other methods have been reported to improve CDT efficacy through GSH depletion, such as the introduction of chemical inhibitors [27,48] and high-valence transition metals [49]. These methods, however, cannot effectively monitor the special physiological process of GSH depletion. Compared with these methods, carbon quantum dots (CDs) not only are simple to prepare but also endow CDT reagents with the function of fluorescence imaging. In future work, the authors will seek to improve the efficacy of CDT through further modification of CDs.

Jun Li: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Zu-E Hu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yun-Jie We: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yan-Hong Liu: Formal analysis, Validation. Na Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiao-Qi Yu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No: 21877082) and the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2018YFA0901600). We also acknowledge the Comprehensive Training Platform of Specialized Laboratory, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University. We also appreciate Shuguang Yan from the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for providing help with XPS characterization.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.08.114.
